Advice No. A-83-120 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Thank you for your request for advice on che conflict of 
1nterests provisions of tne PolItical Reform Act. 
Because the 
actions in question have already occurred, I can only provide 
you with a general explanation of the applicable provisions of 
the Act. 
The facts, as stated In your letter, are that you are 
a City Councilmember for the City of Perris. 
The City 
contracted with Riverside County for fire protection. 
A group 
opposing this agreement cla1ms that you had a conflict of 
interest when you participated in the decision on the 
agreement. 
Their allegation 1S based on the fact that you 
worked for the California Department of Forestry, in their 
Volunteers In Prevention Program, and you were paid $127.20, in 
1981, and $24.55, in 1982. 
Government COde Sect10n 871001/ prohibits a public 
official from making, or participating in the making of a 
governmental decision in which she knows or has reason to know 
she has a financial 1nterest. 
An official has a "financial 
Interest" in a decision witnin the meaning of Section 87100 if 
it is reasonably foreseeable that the deCIsion will mater1ally 
affect a source of income aggregating $250 or more received by 
the public official within 12 montns prior to the time when the 
decision is made. 
(Section 87103 (c) .) 
Thus, if a source of income did not proviae an official with 
at least $250 In income within the last 12 months, the official 
11

has no need to disqualify herself on decisions affecting that 
source. 
In addition, salary receivea from a governmental agency is 
excluaed from tne Act's definition of "income." 
Thus, if 
payments qualify as "salary," the governmental agency is not 
considered to oe a source of income, regardless of the amount of 
payments provided to the official. 
If you have any aaditional questions, please feel free to 
contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
Very truly yours,

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a City Councilmember has a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act when participating in a decision regarding a city fire protection contract, given that the councilmember previously received minimal compensation ($127.20 in 1981 and $24.55 in 1982) from the California Department of Forestry's Volunteers in Prevention Program.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A public official has a financial interest requiring disqualification only if a decision will materially affect a source of income aggregating $250 or more within the preceding 12 months. Additionally, salary from governmental agencies is excluded from the Act's definition of income. The councilmember's minimal payments from the Department of Forestry do not meet the $250 threshold and likely qualify as salary, thus creating no conflict of interest.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains no explicit QUESTION or CONCLUSION section headers, but letter structure clearly presents the legal question and FPPC's analytical response. Advice is responsive to the requestor's factual scenario. Minor OCR artifacts present but do not impede meaning.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a City Councilmember has a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act when participating in a decision regarding a city fire protection contract, given that the councilmember previously received minimal compensation ($127.20 in 1981 and $24.55 in 1982) from the California Department of Forestry's Volunteers in Prevention Program.

CONCLUSION: A public official has a financial interest requiring disqualification only if a decision will materially affect a source of income aggregating $250 or more within the preceding 12 months. Additionally, salary from governmental agencies is excluded from the Act's definition of income. The councilmember's minimal payments from the Department of Forestry do not meet the $250 threshold and likely qualify as salary, thus creating no conflict of interest.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to Pauline Rusher, a City Councilmember for Perris, regarding allegations of conflict of interest in a city decision on a fire protection contract. The FPPC explains that no conflict exists because the councilmember's payments from the state Department of Forestry fell below the $250 threshold and likely qualify as excludable governmental salary.
